item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kiara's dialogue with the stilted grammar is supposed to be descriptive. Is it possible to put a "[sic]" suggesting the punctuation is that way to convey how they're speaking? That seems to be the main issue with this, but those sections are intentional. I guess I just need to use proper grammar and suggest that she's out of it in the italics. But I'm not really into that.

I'll see what else I can do with this conceptually, but that's always hard because these types of articles that I write are meant to be interpretive and not something you have to even figure out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I write dialogue that way sometimes not grammatically correct so you get a better idea of how they're actually talking. In this case kind of hurried and breathless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It replaces everything as soon as it infects it. But there's no real way to tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll get around to this I'm just a little busy. Thanks for the reviews everyone.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this, even if it’s a little rough around the edges in some technical aspects - but I feel like those need to be ironed out a bit before I can feel confident upvoting.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

On her coat is a badge identifying her as Kiara Scott, an Oncologist at a local medical center.

I’m not 100% sure, but I feel like ‘Oncologist’ doesn’t need to be capitalized here.

SCP-4728-A-1

The grammar here is really wacky, with sections like “Hey there Circuit Breakers today we're going to talk about DVDs which is a new format and people are using it to store all of their real life movies on it, and animated movies as well.” and “Well the thing is you never actually take things out of storage! when you "take" something out of the disk, that something gets deleted.” really needing some more punctuation and breaking up a little (The ‘when’ after storage should be capitalized here as well.)

SCP-4728-A-2

I really like the way Kiara talks about moving data here - the idea of deleting it being impossible because something can’t go nowhere really gives us the sense that something else is being discussed through the lens of data, even if we don’t know exactly what.

And the running motif of data being moved around - even if I'm not 100% on what it actually means - definitely gives off the sense that there's something deeper being talked about here, and that sense of intrigue really helps the article.

Novote for now.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Tanhony or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: damn
item.content:encoded: god this place's rating is a warzone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehh.. I just don't really like it, I feel like there's something I'm missing, some weird logic behind the way the virus works and how Kiara speaks, and that if I knew more about computers I would get it. But since I don't know about computers, it just doesn't work for me

By the way, I think you forgot to tag the article, it's on the new untagged pages feed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting concept, but definitely has some reworking that needs to be done. This feels like it's just 1 draft away from the final version, but that one draft definitely makes the article a bit cumbersome. Lots of good ideas, presentation is a bit spotty tho

item.title: Re: Why isn’t the place redacted?
item.content:encoded:
Generally, unnecessary redactions of that sort are discouraged for authors. It may come off as an author skipping parts of the story out of laziness or withholding details just because.

In-universe I'd imagine they'd have tight informational security for who gets to view each file.

item.title: Why isn’t the place redacted?
item.content:encoded: The only problem I had with this was the place not being redacted. I’m pretty sure the Foundation wants to keep these SCPs hidden. Anybody who doesn’t what [REDACTED] means isn’t supposed to know. The Foundation doesn’t want anybody, who somehow gets to their database, to mess with the SCPs. Not redacting a location of a SCP kinda contradicts this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s an interesting article but some things let me to novote this.

Hey there Circuit Breakers today were going to
talk about DVDs which…

The flow feels weird, it needs some commas after Breakers and DVDs.

It also misses tags, which are important if I want to find an article like this.

The idea is simple and the replace gimmick is fun, will probably upvote if fixed but for now a novote

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the images used in this article come from here, here, and here, and are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, but there are a few things that led me to downvote.

Kiara looks to an infographic that is obscured by an instances of SCP-4728.

an instances should be "an instance"

Hey there Circuit Breakers today were going to talk about DVDs which is a new format and people are using it to store all of their real life movies on it, and animated movies as well.

This sounds unnatural and could use some commas, such as after 'breakers' and 'DVDs'. Also, "were going to talk" should be "we're going to talk."

There are some other miscellaneous grammar issues throughout the logs as well. I'd advise you read the logs back to yourself, out loud, and try to make it sound more natural, like adding pauses where pauses sound nice, all that. Except for the areas where it shouldn't sound natural, of course.

That being said, I'm not a huge fan of the whole "Replace" gimmick of linking back to the same article. I understand it, but… eh. Just not doing it for me. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No idea why, but this really worked for me. Very well done.
